acceptance_test: After the revised equivalent of snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:173-175, the chapter must still show four observable beats on the page: Maren has partially sealed the fracture, the attempt has left him too damaged to try again immediately, he refuses the easier outward break because it would endanger real people downhill, and Aldric sends him toward a next crack that creates a new operational burden. Fail if the ending still contains a clean thesis line equivalent to snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:205 or an abstract summary beat equivalent to snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:245 instead of landing on concrete consequence.
problem: The chapter's meaning lands physically when Maren chooses inward at the fissure and the Woundmouth visibly calms, but the aftermath keeps translating that same choice into cleaner and cleaner thesis language. By the time the scene arrives at 'People need marks,' 'this isn't a noble turn,' 'You are choosing maintenance,' and the abstract closing summary about the wall always leaking, the chapter has shifted from revelation to certification. That weakens the eerie force of the reveal and creates avoidable end-of-chapter sprawl.
rewrite_direction: Rewrite and compress the aftermath in snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:183-246. Keep the concrete dramatic work that must remain after the seal lands: Maren's near-death cost, his specific downstream reason for not breaking the seam, Aldric's new ability to identify other fractures, and Maren's decision to walk toward the next one. Cut or roughen the abstract paraphrases in snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:195-205 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:245 so the bowl, the slowed bleed, the route, and the body carry the meaning instead of repeated thematic gloss.

acceptance_test: In the revised versions of snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:39-40, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:135, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:175, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:219, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:233, each cited beat must still convey the same narrative fact by ordinary grammatical narration: the bowl now feels like a real tool, Maren chooses the inward seal, he has only partly succeeded at terrible bodily cost, he is barely alive, and the bowl now sits differently in his hands. Fail if any of those beats still relies on a standalone clipped fragment omitting the subject or necessary verb.
problem: The chapter repeatedly falls into clipped narration fragments for emphasis: 'Different man holding it,' 'Inward. Through him. Seal it,' 'Part of one,' 'Barely ... but alive,' 'Same heft. Less pretending.' Because this pattern recurs at major beats, it reads as thriller-style cadence rather than Maren's canonical past-tense narration. The constitution explicitly forbids elliptical clipping in narration, and the recurrence makes the drift visible instead of incidental.
rewrite_direction: Replace the clipped fragments in snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:39-40, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:135, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:175, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:219, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_17.md:233 with fully grammatical narration or tightly linked clauses. Preserve the urgency and compression, but make the pressure come from rhythm, image, and embodied detail rather than subjectless or verbless sentence shards.

acceptance_test: Pass if the revised material built around snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:9, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:25, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:37, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:175 demonstrates Aldric's softer cadence through scene action and dialogue, with no more than one separate interpretive gloss naming the change afterward. Fail if the revision still pauses after each beat to explain that his pauses, jokes, or tone are different now.
problem: The chapter keeps footnoting Aldric's softened register after the dialogue has already made it legible. Because the boundary pack calls for this change to stay subtle, the repeated explanatory glosses about his pauses, unsolicited observations, gentler humor, and quieter consumption voice flatten the opening into commentary instead of letting the altered relationship live on the page.
rewrite_direction: Trim or recomposition the interpretive follow-ups at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:19; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:27; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:43; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:175 so the pauses, unprompted readings, and gentler joke do more of the work themselves. Keep Maren's awareness of the change, but concentrate it into one sharper observation attached to a specific beat instead of re-explaining each new example.

acceptance_test: Pass if the revised assignment at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:79 no longer uses the clipped scaffold of direction, twelve-mile distance, farm/farmhouse label, and child count in sequence, and instead foregrounds a different practical concern before at least one of those facts. problem: Thessa's assignment line echoes a calibration-only style-bible example too closely: same eastward route, same twelve-mile distance, same farmstead household with children in clipped sequence. Because this lands inside the chapter-defining beat where she acknowledges Maren as working help, the echo damages voice integrity instead of merely sounding derivative.
rewrite_direction: Rewrite the assignment delivery at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:79 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:85 so the information arrives in a materially different order and with chapter-specific operational emphasis. Thessa can stay blunt, but the line needs a fresh syntax and priority structure rather than route-plus-distance-plus-farmhouse-plus-children as a checklist.

acceptance_test: Pass if at least two of the four cited exchanges no longer follow Aldric report -> Maren paraphrase or tease -> Aldric corrective button, and at least one revised exchange changes leverage by adding new emotional or tactical information. The rhythm is initially pleasurable, but by the latter half of the chapter it starts to feel pre-shaped rather than discovered in the moment, which reduces leverage shifts and makes the buddy register more uniform than lived.
rewrite_direction: Rework at least two of the exchange clusters anchored at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:11; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:39; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:177; snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:239 so they do not all resolve as report, paraphrase, correction. acceptance_test: Pass if the revised closing after snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:223 ends on a concrete road, bowl, hum, or fork image and contains no abstract sentence equivalent to the current general claim at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:235. Fail if the revision still explains in general terms what the world needs after the clean yard, payment, and waving child have already made the chapter's point legible.
problem: The closing lands its meaning concretely through the meal, the clean water, the child in the doorway, and the audible life returning to Barren Rill, then paraphrases that meaning in abstract thesis language. Once the chapter has already proved Maren's new ordinary work on the ground, the line about what the world 'needed' and the retrospective bowl summary start re-stating the arrival instead of sharpening it.
rewrite_direction: Compress or replace the abstract coda at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:235 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:245. Preserve the must-land beats from snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:223 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_18.md:245, but let the ending close on one fresh concrete image or action at the road fork rather than a thematic paraphrase of what sin-eaters are for.

acceptance_test: Every bounty-amount reference in the manuscript resolves to a single consistent figure matching the continuity sheet, or any intentional discrepancy is narratively justified at point of introduction.
evidence: Ch3 establishes the bounty as 'four thousand gold crowns' (lines 763, 793), consistent with the continuity sheet's '4,000-gold-crown bounty.' Ch4 reduces it to 'four hundred gold' at lines 906, 1092, and 1172. The amount drops by an order of magnitude with no narrative explanation.
problem: The bounty—Maren's core financial motivation and the story's primary external stakes driver—contradicts itself between Ch3 and Ch4. Four thousand crowns versus four hundred gold is not a rounding error; it changes the scale of consequence. Every subsequent reference to the bounty inherits whichever wrong number the reader last encountered.
rewrite_direction: Standardize all bounty references in Ch4 (lines 906, 1092, 1172) to 'four thousand gold crowns,' matching Ch3 and the continuity sheet. Audit Ch5-18 for any further bounty-amount references and reconcile. If a plot reason exists for a different figure (e.g., a partial bounty or a local price on Maren's head distinct from the Temple bounty), make that distinction explicit in dialogue or narration at first occurrence.

acceptance_test: Any in-narration arithmetic involving the bounty produces a result that is derivable from 4,000 gold crowns with explicit reasoning, and no unexplained third figure appears.
evidence: Line 2231: Maren calculates the bounty as 'four hundred times' multiplied by three gold, yielding 1,200 gold. This matches neither 4,000 crowns (Ch3/continuity sheet) nor 400 gold (Ch4). The arithmetic itself is opaque—'four hundred times' times three is 1,200, not 400 or 4,000.
problem: A third bounty figure surfaces through in-narration math that contradicts both prior amounts. The reader who has been tracking the number now has three incompatible values. This compounds the Ch4 error and suggests the bounty amount drifted during drafting without reconciliation.
rewrite_direction: Replace the line-2231 calculation with arithmetic consistent with the canonical bounty amount (4,000 gold crowns). If Maren is mentally converting or discounting the bounty (e.g., estimating his share, subtracting bribes, converting currencies), make the conversion logic explicit so the reader can follow the math. The final figure must trace back to 4,000 crowns.

acceptance_test: Every character named in Ch15's death report was introduced with at least one identifying detail in Ch14. No character introduced in Ch14 disappears without accounting. No distinguishing trait is attributed to a character other than the one who displayed it.
evidence: Ch14 names five sin-eaters at the Crook: Thessa, Kael, Petra, Errin, and Hale (introduced across lines 3540-3700). The continuity sheet specifies five sin-eaters. Ch15's death report (line 4057-4059) accounts for six people—survivors Thessa, Kael, Hale; dead Petra, Ruald, Denna—naming two characters (Ruald, Denna) who were never introduced in Ch14 and dropping Errin entirely.
problem: The roster breaks between chapters. Ruald and Denna appear only as names in a casualty list, denying the reader any basis for registering their deaths as losses. Errin, who was introduced and given scene presence in Ch14, vanishes without explanation. The root-chewing detail established as Kael's distinguishing gesture (Ch14, line 3596) is attributed to dead Ruald in Ch15 (line 4059), transplanting a living character's trait onto a character the reader never met.
rewrite_direction: Option A (preferred): Introduce all six sin-eaters in Ch14—Thessa, Kael, Petra, Errin, Ruald, and Denna—each with at least one distinguishing detail (gesture, voice tic, physical marker). Root-chewing belongs to whichever character dies with it. Errin must either survive into Ch15's accounting or die on-page. Option B: Reduce Ch15's death list to characters actually introduced in Ch14 (Petra and Errin die; Thessa, Kael, Hale survive). This requires rewriting Thessa's loss-reaction in Ch15-16 to match a smaller body count. Either option must reconcile with the continuity sheet's character count.

acceptance_test: Ch15's death report names only characters introduced in Ch14. All distinguishing details in the death report match the character who displayed them. Survivor + dead count equals Ch14's total roster.
evidence: Lines 4057-4059: 'Petra, Ruald, and Denna didn't make it. Kael's alive—barely. Hale too.' Ruald is given the root-chewing trait (line 4059) that Ch14 line 3596 established as Kael's. The continuity sheet's state_transitions for Thessa reference 'Loses Petra, Ruald, and Denna,' suggesting the names were planned but never planted in Ch14's text.
problem: The death report references a planned roster that was never fully written into the preceding chapter. This is a draft-state artifact: the outline knew about six sin-eaters, but the prose only introduced five, and the wrong five. The misattributed root-chewing detail compounds the error by creating a false continuity—implying the reader should remember Ruald chewing root, when in fact Kael was the one who did it.
rewrite_direction: After fixing Ch14's roster (see CH14_INCOMPLETE_ROSTER), revise Ch15 lines 4057-4059 to ensure: (a) every named dead character appeared on-page in Ch14, (b) the root-chewing detail belongs to the character who displayed it in Ch14, (c) the survivor count matches Ch14's roster minus the dead. If Kael chews root in Ch14 and survives in Ch15, do not attribute root-chewing to a dead character in the death report.

acceptance_test: At least one sin-eater death is rendered through concrete sensory detail (body, object, physical reaction) rather than reported in a casualty list. Surviving characters' grief registers through changed behavior or specific physical response, not summary emotion.
evidence: Lines 4057-4059 deliver three sin-eater deaths (Petra, Ruald, Denna) plus aftermath of the chapter's major confrontation in a brief reported-speech montage. The deaths are conveyed as a list item in Thessa's dialogue rather than experienced as events with embodied consequence.
problem: Five chapters (Ch10-14) build toward the Crook confrontation and the sin-eater community's exposure to real danger. The payoff compresses three named deaths into a casualty roster delivered in dialogue, violating the constitution's anti-sanitization mandate (rule 10) and consequence-rendering requirement (rule 12). The reader invests in these characters' world but receives a summary where embodied aftermath should be. This is not restraint intensifying pressure—it is compression evacuating it.
rewrite_direction: Expand lines 4057-4059 into a passage that renders at least one death with sensory presence—a body detail, a survivor's physical reaction, a specific object left behind. The other deaths can land through Thessa's changed behavior or a concrete aftermath detail (not 'she was devastated'). The passage need not be long; a paragraph of embodied specificity outweighs a page of summary. Model: the constitution's grief example (grocery-list detail) rather than the forbidden 'devastated by the loss' pattern.

acceptance_test: Ch14's opening confrontation does not replicate Ch7's expose-the-fraud structure. Thessa's skepticism, if present, takes a new form or serves a new narrative function.
evidence: Ch14's Crook arrival recapitulates Ch7's dynamic: Thessa confronts Maren's fraudulence, demands credentials he lacks, and exposes him before peers. Ch7 (lines 1700-1750) runs the same pattern—Thessa's examination, Maren's deflection, public exposure of incompetence. Ch14 eventually reaches new ground (Maren's genuine contamination-clearing work), but the first half restages familiar conflict.
problem: The reader experiences diminishing tension because the confrontation shape is recycled. Thessa-exposes-Maren is a one-time reveal; a second staging needs a fundamentally different power dynamic or new information to justify the structural repetition. The chapter does pivot, but the restaged portion occupies significant space before the pivot arrives.
rewrite_direction: Compress or reframe the Ch14 confrontation opening so that Thessa's skepticism manifests differently than in Ch7. Options: (a) Thessa has already accepted Maren's presence and the tension is over what he'll be asked to do, not whether he belongs; (b) another sin-eater challenges Maren while Thessa unexpectedly defends him, inverting the Ch7 dynamic; (c) the confrontation is cut to a few lines of callback ('The same look she'd given him in the examination room') before moving to new territory. The goal is to avoid re-earning credibility that was already at stake in Ch7.

acceptance_test: Thessa's Ch7 examination dialogue does not reproduce the tripartite show-me/show-me/what-can-you-do structure from the style bible example. The scene achieves the same dramatic function through a different rhetorical approach.
evidence: Lines 1721-1729: Thessa's examination dialogue uses the structure 'Show me a containment pass' / 'A residual clear' / 'A density assessment'—a tripartite credential-demand pattern that closely mirrors the style bible's Thessa example line: 'Show me the containment pass. No? Then show me the residual clear. No? What exactly do you know how to do?' The style bible marks all example lines as 'CALIBRATION ONLY — DO NOT COPY,' and the constitution (rule 2b) prohibits reproducing or closely paraphrasing example lines.
problem: The manuscript's Thessa dialogue echoes the style bible's example line in structure, vocabulary, and escalation pattern. While not a verbatim copy, it reproduces the rhetorical shape (show-me X / show-me Y / what can you do) closely enough to constitute the kind of paraphrase the constitution forbids. This suggests the example line leaked into the draft as a template rather than serving its intended calibration function.
rewrite_direction: Rewrite Thessa's examination sequence (lines 1721-1729) to convey the same credential-checking function through a different rhetorical structure. Thessa can still be demanding and methodical, but the specific pattern of 'Show me X. No? Show me Y. No? What do you actually know?' must be replaced. Options: she could run a practical test rather than asking for certifications, or she could challenge Maren's knowledge indirectly through a case-study question, or she could simply observe his technique and comment on its failures.

acceptance_test: The Ch6 Aldric/Vess argument does not replicate Ch5's accusation-deflection-counter-concession structure. The scene either introduces new stakes, shifts the conflict axis, or compresses the familiar beats to under five exchanges before pivoting.
evidence: Lines 1488-1508: Aldric and Vess argue about trust, obligation, and Maren's reliability in a pattern that recycles Ch5's conversational structure—accusation, deflection, moral counter, reluctant concession. The specific beats (Aldric appeals to duty, Vess questions Maren's motives, Aldric concedes a point, temporary truce) mirror the Ch5 argument's shape.
problem: Two consecutive chapters run the same argument template between the same characters without sufficient escalation or new information to justify the repetition. The reader registers this as spinning wheels rather than advancing conflict. The constitution's rule 9 (recurring prose formulas) extends to dialogue templates that repeat structural shapes across scenes.
rewrite_direction: Revise the Ch6 argument (lines 1488-1508) to either (a) introduce new information that changes the power dynamic between Aldric and Vess, (b) shift the argument to a different axis of conflict (e.g., tactical disagreement rather than moral disagreement), or (c) compress the exchange so the familiar beats pass quickly and the scene pivots to new territory. The goal is for Ch6's conflict to feel like escalation from Ch5, not repetition of it.

acceptance_test: The passage from lines 950-970 contains at most one reference to the duration or fraudulence of Maren's past rituals. The remaining text focuses on the present-tense attempt and its physical consequences.
evidence: Ch1 line 9 establishes the fraud ('pretending to eat sins'); by Ch5 the phrase 'eleven years' has appeared 20+ times in Chs 1-4. Ch5 lines 950 and 954 restate it in consecutive paragraphs ('the thing he'd been pretending to do for eleven years'; 'For eleven years, every ritual Maren had performed was about closing'), and line 966 adds 'a thousand fake rituals' restating the same point a third time within 16 lines.
problem: Maren's fraudulent history is re-proved three times in a dense exposition block (lines 950-966) without materially new information. The reader has encountered this baseline 20+ times already. First established Ch1 line 9. Manuscript total: 45 occurrences of 'eleven years' across 16 chapters.
rewrite_direction: Keep ONE of the three restatements in the consumption-attempt passage (line 950 is strongest because it transitions into action). Cut the expository paragraph at line 954 entirely — the reader knows this. Rephrase line 966 to focus on the muscle-memory reflex without re-explaining that the rituals were fake.

acceptance_test: Chapter 8's opening three pages contain zero uses of the phrase 'eleven years' and no expository explanation of how System-sighted perception differs from Maren's.
evidence: Ch8 line 1916: 'faking his way across Fenhold's farmland'; line 1928: 'Eleven years of contamination in named villages, children growing up in the residue of quests he'd only pretended to clear'; line 1952: 'Eleven years of performing a System role'; line 1926: 'The difference was that the System-sighted saw it with data overlaid — quest details, threat levels, completion percentages — and Maren saw the raw thing.' System-blindness formula near-identical to Ch4 line 868. Adjacent to Ch7 which contains 5 'eleven years' uses.
problem: Ch8 opening bundles three redundant baselines: (1) the fraud premise at line 1916, (2) 'eleven years' twice at lines 1928 and 1952, and (3) the System-blindness perceptual formula at line 1926 restating the 'everyone sees data, Maren sees raw light' contrast established at Ch4 line 868. All three are well-established by Ch8.
rewrite_direction: Line 1916: replace the fraud-recap clause with a sensory detail about the road. Line 1926: cut the explanatory contrast ('The difference was that the System-sighted saw it with data overlaid...'); by Ch8 the reader understands Maren's blindness. Line 1928: keep the guilt-weight but cut 'Eleven years of contamination in named villages' — replace with a specific image. Line 1952: cut 'Eleven years of performing a System role.'

acceptance_test: Chapter 9 contains zero instances of 'eleven years' in narrator exposition. The market-deception scene conveys Maren's skill through action.
evidence: Ch9 line 2343: 'he said it the way he'd said a thousand things over eleven years of fraud'; line 2349: 'The lie was smooth enough after eleven years.' Adjacent to Ch8 (2 uses at lines 1928, 1952).
problem: The fraud-duration baseline is re-proved twice more in Ch9 narration (lines 2343, 2349), immediately adjacent to Ch8 which also restates it twice. By Ch9, the manuscript has used 'eleven years' 35+ times.
rewrite_direction: Line 2343: keep the lie-delivery action but cut 'over eleven years of fraud.' Line 2349: cut 'after eleven years' entirely; the smoothness of the lie is self-evident from context.

acceptance_test: The bowl reference at line 3102 does not restate its duration of use or its status as a prop.
evidence: Ch12 line 3102: 'Same chipped rim. Same market glaze. Same stupid prop he'd used for eleven years.' By Ch12 the phrase has appeared 39 times in prior chapters.
problem: The bowl-as-prop baseline and eleven-year duration are re-proved in Ch12 narration despite 39 prior uses. First established Ch1 line 9; restated in Ch2 line 388, Ch5 line 966, and elsewhere.
rewrite_direction: Cut 'he'd used for eleven years' from the bowl description. The 'chipped rim' and 'market glaze' already identify the bowl. If the fraud context is needed, replace with a forward-looking beat.

acceptance_test: The passage between lines 1603-1607 does not contain an expository explanation of what a real sin-eater does versus what Maren does.
evidence: Ch7 line 1605: 'a real one, the kind who actually cleared contamination instead of performing clearance while the contamination sat untouched in the soil underneath...someone who'd spent a lifetime doing what Maren had spent a lifetime pretending to do.' First established Ch1 lines 9-33.
problem: The narrator restates the real-vs-fake sin-eater premise as a full paragraph (line 1605) in Ch7, as if the reader needs reminding of the novel's central conceit by Chapter 7 of 18. The Thessa dialogue lines (1695, 1713, 1829) earn their restatements through dramatic confrontation; the narration at 1605 does not.
rewrite_direction: Cut or compress line 1605 to a single clause. Replace the explanatory aside with Maren's emotional state or a sensory detail from the market approach. Let Thessa's dialogue do the work of contrasting real and fake.

acceptance_test: Chapter 14 contains at most one reference to Thessa's stained hands and zero uses of 'thirty years' in connection to Thessa's experience.
evidence: Ch14 line 3572: 'a ceramic bowl in her stained hands'; line 3652: 'stained dark, edges worn smooth, a tool that had seen thirty years of genuine use'; line 3724: 'Thessa's stained hands'; line 3596: Kael's hands described via comparison to Thessa's staining. First established Ch7 lines 1637, 1657, 1673, 1777.
problem: Thessa's stained hands are mentioned 4 times within Ch14 (lines 3572, 3596, 3652, 3724) plus 'thirty years' at line 3652. The trait was established 5 times in Ch7. Total: 9+ instances of this single physical descriptor.
rewrite_direction: Keep ONE stained-hands reference in Ch14 (line 3572 on first reappearance is strongest). Cut staining from lines 3652 and 3724. At line 3596, describe Kael's hands independently. Cut 'thirty years' from line 3652.

acceptance_test: Chapter 15's opening Thessa description does not use the word 'stained' or reference hand discoloration.
evidence: Ch15 line 3901: 'her stained hands were empty.' Adjacent to Ch14 which mentions stained hands 4 times (lines 3572, 3596, 3652, 3724).
problem: Thessa's stained hands re-described at Ch15 opening (line 3901), immediately adjacent to Ch14's 4-mention cluster. First established Ch7 line 1637.
rewrite_direction: Replace 'her stained hands were empty' with a different physical detail conveying the same information (she put something down in a hurry). Use posture, expression, or movement instead of staining.

acceptance_test: Line 4648 does not contain the phrase 'thirty years.' Thessa's hands may be referenced without restating career duration.
evidence: Ch18 line 4648: 'Her hands, stained dark from thirty years of ritual contamination exposure, made the fruit look very white.' Ch7 line 1679: 'hers dark with thirty years of absorbed contamination'; Ch14 line 3652: 'thirty years of genuine use.'
problem: The 'stained hands + thirty years' combination appears for the third time at Ch18 line 4648. The full descriptor package was established in Ch7 line 1679 and restated in Ch14 line 3652.
rewrite_direction: Keep the visual contrast ('made the fruit look very white') but cut 'from thirty years of ritual contamination exposure.' The word 'stained' alone or 'her dark hands' is sufficient.

acceptance_test: Chapter 16 does not contain the phrase 'order that no longer existed' or any close synonym.
evidence: Ch16 line 4308: 'A report delivered to an order that no longer existed.' Also at Ch2 line 552 (first use), Ch4 line 848, Ch9 line 2461, Ch14 line 3546, Ch17 line 4483. Total: 6 uses of the exact formula across 6 chapters.
problem: Aldric's 'reports filed to an order that no longer existed' is a fixed formula used 6 times verbatim. The Ch17 use (line 4483) functions as deliberate dissolution of the motif; the Ch16 use at line 4308 is a plain restatement that dulls the Ch17 payoff.
rewrite_direction: At line 4308, cut the 'to an order that no longer existed' formula. Convey Aldric's formality with fresh language. Reserve the formula's final appearance for Ch17 line 4483 where it is deliberately broken.

acceptance_test: Chapter 9 does not describe Vess's voice as 'flat' or 'professional.' Her style is conveyed through speech content and action.
evidence: Ch9 line 2421: 'The word was flat, professional, a field assessment delivered without emphasis.' Adjacent to Ch8 lines 1980 ('flat, precise, billing by the syllable'), 1994 ('flat as ever'), 2118 ('Vess's flat delivery'). Total: 12+ uses of 'flat' for Vess across 8 chapters.
problem: Vess's voice is tagged as 'flat' in nearly every chapter she appears in (12+ uses across Ch4, Ch8, Ch9, Ch10, Ch14, Ch16, Ch17). The adjacent Ch8-Ch9 cluster (3+1 uses) is the most jarring. First established Ch4 line 1046. The pattern blunts the payoff when her flatness breaks in Ch15.
rewrite_direction: At line 2421, convey Vess's urgency through speech content and brevity rather than re-tagging her voice. Cut the 'flat, professional' descriptor. More broadly, limit 'flat' as a Vess tag to 4-5 total uses to preserve impact.

acceptance_test: Line 872 presents Derry's nervous chatter through action or dialogue, not through narrator explanation of the behavioral pattern.
evidence: Ch4 line 872: 'He talked as he always did when he was scared: too much and about nothing, each sentence hitched to the next.' Ch3 line 589: 'the leek monologue was a tell — the worse things got, the more Derry talked...The content didn't matter. What mattered was the noise, the filling of space.' Adjacent chapters.
problem: Derry's talking-when-scared behavioral tell is described in full expository form in both Ch3 (line 589) and Ch4 (line 872), adjacent chapters. Both explain the same mechanism: Derry fills silence with chatter when frightened.
rewrite_direction: At line 872, cut the explanatory aside 'as he always did when he was scared: too much and about nothing...' Show Derry talking — the reader will recognize the pattern from Ch3. Line 874 ('Maren let him talk. It was familiar.') already provides sufficient recognition.

acceptance_test: Line 1930 does not re-characterize Thessa's delivery style through a clinical/medical/infrastructure simile.
evidence: Ch8 line 1930: 'Thessa had said it the way she said everything — clinically, as if the fact of his approaching dissolution was a drainage problem and she was describing the pipe.' Ch7 line 1661: 'clipped, clinical — a surgeon cataloguing damage.' Adjacent chapters.
problem: Thessa's clinical voice is re-described in Ch8 (line 1930) with a medical/infrastructure simile, adjacent to Ch7 where it was first established with a parallel simile at line 1661. Both describe the same trait: Thessa delivers devastating information in detached professional register.
rewrite_direction: At line 1930, cut 'the way she said everything — clinically, as if...' and let the quoted content convey Thessa's delivery. The reader met Thessa one chapter ago.

acceptance_test: Chapter 6 contains at most one explicit mention of hand tremor or shaking. The pressing-palms-flat gesture does not appear in Ch6.
evidence: Ch5 lines 1387, 1407, 1417 describe Maren's hand tremor and the 'pressing palms flat against thighs' management gesture. Ch6 lines 1430, 1438 restate both the tremor and the same pressing gesture within the first 20 lines, and lines 1526, 1562, 1564, 1582 continue describing the same tremor throughout. Total: 8+ mentions of the same tremor across Ch5-Ch6 (~200 lines).
problem: Maren's post-fight hand tremor is established in Ch5 and then re-proved 5+ times in adjacent Ch6 without new information.
